topic: How The Moon Is Slowly Drifting Away
title: The Tidal Physics That Pushes the Moon Farther From Earth

lesson.learn: The Moon is drifting away because Earth's tides create a small but persistent transfer of angular momentum. Earth's rotation is slightly slowed by tidal friction, and the Moon gains that energy in the form of a gradually larger orbit. The effect is tiny year to year, but over geological time it becomes significant.
lesson.simpler: Earth's tides act like a slow tug on the Moon. That tug makes the Moon move a little farther away each year.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The Moon is receding because tidal friction transfers angular momentum from Earth's spin into the Moon's orbit.
lesson.deeperDive: Lunar laser ranging has measured the recession rate very precisely, and it is about 3.8 centimeters per year on average. That rate is not constant through deep time, because ocean shapes, continental positions, and tidal resonance all change the efficiency of the tidal brake. The Moon's orbit and Earth's spin are therefore coupled in a long-term exchange that slowly lengthens the day and enlarges the lunar orbit. The process will continue for billions of years, although the exact long-term outcome depends on planetary evolution that is still unfolding.
lesson.funFact: Days on Earth were much shorter in the distant past because the Moon was closer and tidal braking was stronger.
references: NOAA and NASA Earth-Moon system materials

people: George William Hill - early tidal theorist
Modern lunar laser ranging teams - precision measurement
places: Earth's oceans - source of tidal friction
Lunar laser ranging stations - distance measurement sites
The Earth-Moon barycenter - shared orbital center
years: Ancient Earth - strong tides and faster rotation
Present day - recession measured at about 3.8 cm per year
